front: Dynasty: Chinese dynasty that ruled from 618 to 907 CE, succeeding the Sui and eventually giving way to the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Dynasty: its rule was interrupted by Empress Wu Zetian, who founded her own Zhou dynasty
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Dynasty: gravely weakened by the An Lushan Rebellion
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Dynasty: ruled China from the capital city of Chang'an
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Dynasty: regarded as a golden age of Chinese civilization, especially renowned for its poetry
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Ruler: first ruler of his dynasty; rebelled against and defeated the last Sui emperor, aided by the 70,000-strong 'Army of the Lady' raised by his daughter Princess Pingyang
work: Founding: Gaozu, the Xuanwu Gate Incident, and Taizong

front: Figure: dispatched by Princess Pingyang, this non-eunuch servant recruited rural bandits to help raise her 'Army of the Lady'
work: Founding: Gaozu, the Xuanwu Gate Incident, and Taizong

front: Event: in this incident, the dynasty's second ruler killed two brothers, including the Crown Prince, forcing his father to abdicate
work: Founding: Gaozu, the Xuanwu Gate Incident, and Taizong

front: Ruler: won the title Tian Kehan ('Heavenly Khan') after defeating the Eastern Turkic Khanate
work: Founding: Gaozu, the Xuanwu Gate Incident, and Taizong

front: Ruler: a commander in his reign captured Illig Qaghan, ruler of the Eastern Turkic Khaganate, and became chancellor as a result
work: Founding: Gaozu, the Xuanwu Gate Incident, and Taizong

front: Dynasty: after its defeat of the Eastern Turkic Khaganate, Bilge Khan's Orkhon inscription decried the recruitment of Turkic officers into its military
work: Founding: Gaozu, the Xuanwu Gate Incident, and Taizong

front: Ruler: sent the monk Xuanzang on a 16-year pilgrimage from Chang'an to India, where he studied at Nalanda monastery under King Harsha's favor and returned with Buddhist scriptures
work: Founding: Gaozu, the Xuanwu Gate Incident, and Taizong

front: Event: this pagoda, built during the Tang dynasty, housed the Buddhist scriptures the monk Xuanzang brought back from India
work: Founding: Gaozu, the Xuanwu Gate Incident, and Taizong

front: Ruler: as co-founder, was heavily influenced by his time on the frontier and brought Turkic arts and fashions into Chinese court culture
work: Founding: Gaozu, the Xuanwu Gate Incident, and Taizong

front: Ruler: his niece Princess Wencheng married the Tibetan king Songtsen Gampo
work: Founding: Gaozu, the Xuanwu Gate Incident, and Taizong

front: Ruler: declared herself emperor, interrupting Tang rule with her own Zhou dynasty, after her son Ruizong announced that his mother would rule
work: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum

front: Ruler: promulgated the Great Cloud Sutra, which predicted the return of the Maitreya Buddha
work: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum

front: Ruler: had monks write a commentary declaring her the reincarnation of the Devi of Pure Radiance to legitimize her rule
work: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum

front: Ruler: a blank stele in front of the Qianling Mausoleum was meant to eulogize her
work: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum

front: Ruler: earlier ordered the death of her rival Empress Wang
work: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum

front: Ruler: may have killed her own infant daughter in order to frame a rival for the murder
work: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum

front: Ruler: was likely poisoned via a pastry by his wife, the future Empress Wei, in a conspiracy with Princess Anle
work: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum

front: Figure: led the coup that overthrew Empress Wei and Princess Anle after they poisoned Emperor Zhongzong
work: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum

front: Ruler: returned to the throne with the help of his son Longji (the future Xuanzong) and his sister Taiping
work: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum

front: Figure: was later forced to commit suicide after helping her brother Ruizong return to the throne
work: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum

front: Ruler: regarded as the most powerful female ruler in Chinese history
work: Wu Zetian and the Zhou Interregnum

front: Ruler: was infatuated with his consort Yang Guifei, the last of the legendary Four Beauties of China
work: Xuanzong's Reign: the Kaiyuan Golden Age and Yang Guifei

front: Figure: her fuller-figured, round-faced look exemplified this dynasty's beauty standards
work: Xuanzong's Reign: the Kaiyuan Golden Age and Yang Guifei

front: Figure: his longest-serving chancellor, deliberately appointed non-Han generals like An Lushan to jiedushi border posts to protect his own position
work: Xuanzong's Reign: the Kaiyuan Golden Age and Yang Guifei

front: Figure: her cousin Yang Guozhong served as a chancellor nearly as corrupt as Li Linfu
work: Xuanzong's Reign: the Kaiyuan Golden Age and Yang Guifei

front: Figure: had fresh lychees delivered by horseback all the way from Guangdong
work: Xuanzong's Reign: the Kaiyuan Golden Age and Yang Guifei

front: Ruler: constructed China's first performing arts academy, the Pear Garden
work: Xuanzong's Reign: the Kaiyuan Golden Age and Yang Guifei

front: Ruler: established the Intrepid Militia
work: Xuanzong's Reign: the Kaiyuan Golden Age and Yang Guifei

front: Figure: a half-Sogdian, half-Turkic general rumored to be the lover of Yang Guifei, who had even 'adopted' him as her infant 'son'
work: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Yan Dynasty

front: Event: An Lushan founded this rival dynasty during his rebellion against the Tang
work: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Yan Dynasty

front: Figure: An Lushan's successor in the rebellion; his suicide ended the revolt (also called the An Shi Rebellion)
work: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Yan Dynasty

front: Event: the rebellion captured the capitals Luoyang and Chang'an, forcing Emperor Xuanzong to flee toward Sichuan
work: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Yan Dynasty

front: Figure: was executed by Xuanzong while fleeing to Sichuan, amid rumors of her affair with the rebel general An Lushan
work: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Yan Dynasty

front: Figure: his abandonment of defenses led to the capital's capture during the An Lushan Rebellion
work: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Yan Dynasty

front: Ruler: retook the capital from rebel forces with the help of Uighur allies
work: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Yan Dynasty

front: Institution: these autonomous regional military governors first gained unprecedented power in the aftermath of the An Lushan Rebellion
work: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Yan Dynasty

front: Event: many prominent Chinese families bearing the rebel leader's surname, An, changed their names to avoid association with him
work: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Yan Dynasty

front: Event: the dynasty lost territory that today forms this autonomous region of northwestern China in the rebellion's aftermath
work: The An Lushan Rebellion and the Yan Dynasty

front: Event: Tang general Gao Xianzhi's army was defeated by the Abbasid Caliphate here in 751 after Karluk mercenaries defected
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Event: this defeat is traditionally credited with spreading papermaking technology westward, after Chinese prisoners of war leaked the secret at a mill in Samarqand
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Institution: this protectorate, established to control the western frontier, maintained Four Garrison cities — Karashar, Kucha, Hotan, and Kashgar
back: Protectorate General to Pacify the West
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Event: fought the Tibetan Empire in a near-constant series of wars and shifting alliances, losing much of its western holdings to Tibetan expansion
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Ruler: was away from the capital Chang'an when Tibetan Empire troops briefly occupied it in 763
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Event: fought three successive wars against Korea's Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla, ultimately allying with Silla to conquer the other two kingdoms
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Ruler: initially refused to recognize Silla's Queen Seondeok because she was a woman, but later allied with her
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Event: this kingdom repeatedly switched sides in conflicts between the Tang and the Tibetan Empire
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Event: a shipwreck found off this island, carrying Tang luxury goods bound for the Abbasid Caliphate, is the largest hoard of Tang artifacts ever found
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Dynasty: its officials and emissaries became known to Abbasid observers by a nickname referring to their dark garments
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Dynasty: in 750 CE, its capital Chang'an and the Abbasid capital were the two most populous cities in the world
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Event: this Tang victory featured members of the (non-Japanese) Ashina tribe fighting on both sides
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Event: Abbasid ruler Abu Jafar sent mercenaries to help the Tang put down this rebellion
work: Frontier Wars: Tibet, Korea, and the Battle of Talas

front: Event: Emperor Wuzong's Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution also targeted Christianity and Manicheism, which it deemed forms of Buddhism
work: Buddhism, Nestorian Christianity, and Religious Persecution

front: Figure: this Japanese monk was deported during the Great Anti-Buddhist Persecution after lying about his origins and keeping a detailed travel diary
work: Buddhism, Nestorian Christianity, and Religious Persecution

front: Event: the dynasty built the Fengxiansi Cave at this cave-temple complex south of Luoyang, home to 2,300 caves with almost 110,000 Buddhist statues
work: Buddhism, Nestorian Christianity, and Religious Persecution

front: Figure: the first Christian missionary to reach China; his mission is recorded on the Xi'an Stele
work: Buddhism, Nestorian Christianity, and Religious Persecution

front: Figure: this Tang official and founder of Neo-Confucianism criticized the presence of the Buddha's finger bone relic in his essay 'Memorial on the Bone-relics of the Buddha'
work: Buddhism, Nestorian Christianity, and Religious Persecution

front: Institution: this Buddhist savings bank stocked by donations was liquidated by the Tang state
work: Buddhism, Nestorian Christianity, and Religious Persecution

front: Institution: these regional military governors gained unprecedented autonomy, especially after the An Lushan Rebellion
work: Institutions and Economy

front: Institution: this militia system required able-bodied peasants to serve one month out of every five if living within 500 li of the capital
work: Institutions and Economy

front: Institution: this land distribution system, inherited via the Sui, collapsed alongside the Tang amid the rise of autonomous fanzhen warlords
work: Institutions and Economy

front: Institution: this form of paper cheques, developed amid a coin shortage, replaced copper coinage
work: Institutions and Economy

front: Institution: sold merchants the right to buy government-monopolized salt, offsetting lost land-tax revenue
work: Institutions and Economy

front: Event: Tang institutions, including the equal-field system, were adopted wholesale by this set of Japanese reforms
work: Institutions and Economy

front: Dynasty: Korea's Goryeo dynasty later minted its first native coins in imitation of this dynasty's currency
work: Institutions and Economy

front: Dynasty: its era produced the celebrated poets Li Bai, Du Fu, and Wang Wei
work: Poetry: Li Bai, Du Fu, and Wang Wei

front: Author: the 'Saint of Poetry,' who wrote 'Song of the Wagons' recounting his experiences of the An Lushan Rebellion
work: Poetry: Li Bai, Du Fu, and Wang Wei

front: Work: this 18th-century anthology of roughly 300 Tang poems was compiled by a scholar nicknamed the 'retired master'
work: Poetry: Li Bai, Du Fu, and Wang Wei

front: Work: this Li Bai poem describes the speaker's reaction to moonlight and is still memorized by Chinese schoolchildren
work: Poetry: Li Bai, Du Fu, and Wang Wei

front: Author: wrote about drinking alone with his shadow and the moon, tied to the folklore of a jade rabbit
work: Poetry: Li Bai, Du Fu, and Wang Wei

front: Work: this Wang Wei poem, set on a 'lonely mountain,' describes 'rays of sunlight' shining 'upon green moss'
work: Poetry: Li Bai, Du Fu, and Wang Wei

front: Author: pioneered the impressionistic 'Southern School' of shanshui ('mountain-water') landscape painting
work: Poetry: Li Bai, Du Fu, and Wang Wei

front: Work: this Bo Juyi poem was written about the death of Yang Guifei
back: The Song of Everlasting Regret (Bo Juyi)
work: Poetry: Li Bai, Du Fu, and Wang Wei

front: Work: this Gustav Mahler orchestral song cycle set Tang-era poems, including those by Qian Qi, to music, ending with the singer repeating the German word 'ewig'
back: Das Lied von der Erde (Gustav Mahler)

front: Author: this Tang painter, active alongside Wang Wei, is associated with developing the splashed-ink 'pomo' technique

front: Work: this scroll, traditionally attributed to the earlier painter Gu Kaizhi, has also been argued to date to the Tang dynasty

front: Event: this Gansu-province cave, nicknamed for the trove of manuscripts found within it, is a major surviving source of Tang-era painting
work: Painting, Music, and Court Culture

front: Event: Tang nobles often used matches of this sport as opportunities to murder political rivals; Emperor Muzong died in an accident during one
work: Painting, Music, and Court Culture

front: Event: surviving Tang pottery figurines depict women playing this recently introduced sport

front: Event: this style of ceramic glaze, known in the West as 'egg and spinach,' was first introduced to the Middle East during the Tang dynasty

front: Institution: the Tang court's musical ensembles expanded from the Sui's seven to nine, adding a new classification for 'foreign music' called this

front: Work: this Tang-era poem later inspired multi-movement Western compositions

front: Dynasty: Muslim traders first settled in China during this dynasty

front: Ruler: women began fashioning their hair into the 'convict cinch' style in sympathy for this exiled emperor, who fled to Chengdu
